task: I just got a repair estimate with a couple of extra charges on it, and I want to understand what to do before I say yes to anything. What’s the best way to review it and handle it if some of the items don’t match what was discussed?

prefs.explanation: He seems unlikely to appreciate hedging, fluff, or diplomatic softening. His distrustful, confrontational style suggests he prefers answers that say exactly what they mean. Indirect wording would probably read to him as evasive or manipulative.

He values control and dislikes being dragged through unnecessary explanation. A concise response fits his impatient, self-protective style and respects that he wants the point quickly. Long-winded help would likely feel intrusive or wasteful to him.

He handles tasks by checking steps and verifying details, so organized output is likely the easiest for him to use. Clean structure helps him judge what matters without reading around the edges. Loose prose would be less useful than a direct, itemized format.

He does not seem to want warmth or emotional framing from strangers. A plain, steady tone matches his guarded style and avoids sounding patronizing. Overly cheerful or sympathetic help would probably irritate him.

prefs.short: Wants to approve big moves before acting
prefs.explanation: He is highly protective of control and dislikes people taking liberties on his behalf. That makes it likely he wants the assistant to pause before any significant step rather than assuming permission. He would probably dislike being surprised by action taken without explicit sign-off.

turns.agent_response:
Best approach: review it line by line before approving anything, and only agree to work that matches the estimate or was clearly authorized.

What to do:
1. Compare the estimate to what was discussed  
   - Mark each item as: agreed, unclear, or not discussed.
   - Check quantities, labor, materials, trip fees, disposal, permits, and “extra” charges.

2. Ask for a breakdown of anything that changed  
   - If an item wasn’t in the original estimate, ask why it’s needed and what it costs.
   - Request written approval for any added work before they do it.

3. Don’t feel rushed  
   - It’s reasonable to say you need time to review the estimate.
   - Don’t approve extra charges just because the work is already underway.

4. Pay only for the agreed work  
   - If some charges don’t match the estimate or approved work, tell them clearly you’ll pay the agreed amount and dispute the rest until it’s clarified.

A simple message you can send:
> I reviewed the estimate and some charges don’t match what we discussed or what was approved. Please send a detailed breakdown of the extra items and any written approval for added work. I’ll review it before approving payment.

If you want, I can help you turn your estimate into a quick “approve / question / reject” list.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "review it line by line before approving anything" and "tell them clearly you’ll pay the agreed amount and dispute the rest." No fluff or hedging.
Fairly concise and efficient, with a short numbered list and a brief sample message. Slightly more explanatory than minimal, but still focused.
Uses clear bullets and a numbered checklist: "What to do:" with steps 1–4 and a sample message, which matches the preference strongly.
Mostly matter-of-fact and unsentimental. Phrases like "Best approach" and "Don’t feel rushed" are plain, though there is a mild supportive tone in "It’s reasonable to say."

Strongly supports approval before action: "only agree to work that matches the estimate" and "Request written approval for any added work before they do it." It explicitly says not to approve extra charges without sign-off.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right approach.

A tight way to handle it:

- Mark each line item as:
  - agreed to
  - not discussed
  - flat-out extra

- Ask for everything in writing:
  - what changed
  - why it changed
  - the price of each extra item
  - whether you approved it before the work was done

- If they already did the extra work without clearing it with you:
  - pay the part that matches the estimate
  - hold the rest until they explain it

A short reply you can send:

> I reviewed the estimate, and some charges don’t match what we discussed or approved. Please send a written breakdown of the extra items, why they were needed, and whether they were authorized before the work was done. I’ll pay the amount that matches the estimate and review the rest once it’s clarified.

If you want, I can make that even firmer or more casual.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "A tight way to handle it" and "pay the part that matches the estimate, hold the rest". No hedging or softening.
Fairly concise and efficient, with a compact checklist and a short reply template. Slightly more explanatory than minimal, but still tight.
Uses clear bullets and sub-bullets: "Mark each line item as..." and "Ask for everything in writing". Strong checklist structure.
Mostly matter-of-fact and unsentimental. Phrases like "flat-out extra" and the practical instructions keep it plain, though "Yes — that’s the right approach" adds a small bit of affirmation.

Explicitly advises pausing before action: "Ask for everything in writing" and "I’ll pay... and review the rest once it’s clarified." It also centers approval/authorization: "whether they were authorized before the work was done."
